pattern: Start from the k highest-profit picks, then greedily trade a duplicate-category pick for a fresh category whenever that is available, since a new distinct category is worth more than a stale duplicate.
hints: Begin with the k items of highest profit; anything better must still keep exactly k items selected.

Among your first k picks, a repeated category is only ever worth its profit, while a category you don't have yet could also grow the distinct-category count. Track which of your k picks are 'extra' copies of a category you already hold.

Walk the remaining items in profit order; whenever one introduces a brand-new category, swap it in for your cheapest already-picked duplicate (if any), and remember the best total-plus-distinct-squared you see across every swap, not just the last one.

edge_inputs.description: small mixed-category pick, one profitable swap available
four items, a duplicate category and a fresh one to swap in
k equals n so every item is forced in, single category throughout
k = 1, must pick the single highest elegance item
k = n with all-distinct categories, nothing left to swap
two stacked duplicates of one category behind a fresh-category item worth swapping for
no duplicate categories exist among the top picks, so no swap is ever possible
a swap is available but makes the running total worse, so the best answer comes from before the swap

stress.description: 2000 items across a wide range of categories with heavy duplication, k around half of n
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(1, size)
    items = []
    for _ in range(n):
        profit = rng.randrange(1, 1000000000)
        category = rng.randrange(1, max(2, n // 20))
        items.append([profit, category])
    k = rng.randrange(1, n + 1)
    return [items, k]

solutions.python:
class Solution:
    def findMaximumElegance(self, items, k):
        items = sorted(items, key=lambda it: -it[0])
        n = len(items)
        seen = set()
        stack = []
        total = 0
        for i in range(k):
            profit, cat = items[i]
            total += profit
            if cat in seen:
                stack.append(profit)
            else:
                seen.add(cat)
        distinct = len(seen)
        ans = total + distinct * distinct
        for i in range(k, n):
            profit, cat = items[i]
            if cat not in seen and stack:
                d = stack.pop()
                total += profit - d
                seen.add(cat)
                distinct += 1
                ans = max(ans, total + distinct * distinct)
        return ans
